title: Reputation, Reliability & Work Others Depend On

required.title: [For Student] A project journal or log

supplementary.title: [For Student] Biographies of people known for reliability

competence_markers.description: They can explain how reputation is built and lost, with real examples — not just reciting the concept, but demonstrating understanding through application
Their commitment log shows a reliability rate that is improving over time — the percentage of commitments met, on time, at standard is trending upward
They communicate proactively when a commitment is at risk — they do not wait until the deadline passes to explain why they missed it
They have successfully contributed to real-stakes projects where others depended on their work — and delivered
They have practiced the full repair cycle at least once — acknowledged failure, made it right, and changed behavior
They show awareness of how their behavior affects others — not just their own outcomes, but the impact on people who were counting on them
They can identify the difference between an excuse and a genuine acknowledgment of failure
They are receiving increased responsibility over time — a natural consequence of demonstrated reliability

narration_prompts.detail: How is it built? How is it lost? Give an example of someone — real or from a book — whose reputation was earned through consistent behavior.

Not "do you want to be reliable" — are you, right now, the person others can count on? What does your commitment log say? Where is the gap between how reliable you think you are and how reliable you actually are?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time your work was part of something larger.
narration_prompts.detail: How did your contribution affect the whole? What would have happened if you had not delivered?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a time you let someone down.
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What did it cost them? What did you do about it? What would you do differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Who is the most reliable person you know?
narration_prompts.detail: What makes them reliable? What do people say about them? How did they earn that reputation?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between an excuse and an explanation?
narration_prompts.detail: When you fail to deliver, what do people actually need from you?

cross_connections.description: Reputation is integrity made visible over time. The person who is honest and consistent in their character (IC1.3) produces the behavioral patterns that build reputation (IC2.5-B). The two sub-domains reinforce each other — integrity without visible reliability is invisible virtue; reliability without integrity is manipulation.

The standard of your work is the substance of your reputation. IC2.2-B teaches you to define and meet standards; IC2.5-B teaches you that those standards are observed by others and become the basis for their trust in you.

Trade work is where reputation becomes most tangible. The plumber whose joints leak, the carpenter whose shelves sag, the electrician whose wiring fails — these are reputation failures with physical consequences. IC2.5-B's principles apply directly to every trade skill the student develops.

Artistic reputation is built the same way — consistent quality, honest effort, and a body of work that demonstrates care over time. The artist who produces one great piece and twenty careless ones has not built a reputation for quality.

Civic trust is collective reputation. A community where people keep their commitments, deliver on their promises, and show up when they say they will is a functioning community. IC2.5-B's individual reliability concepts scale directly to civic life.

content.why_this_matters:
In Stretching Branches, you learned to care for your tools, respect your materials, and finish your work neatly. You learned the quiet pride that comes from making something honest — something that represents your actual effort. Those habits are still yours, and they will be yours for life.

Now the scope expands. Stretching Branches was about you and your work. Weathering Storms is about you, your work, and **other people.**

Here is what changes: when you work alone, the only person affected by the quality of your work is you. If you rush a drawing, you have a rushed drawing. If you leave your tools out, you are the one who cannot find them tomorrow. The consequences are personal and contained.

But the moment other people depend on your work — the moment you are part of a team, a family project, a group task, a commitment someone else is counting on — the stakes change completely. Your reliability becomes something others build their plans around. Your reputation — the pattern of whether you deliver or do not — becomes a real thing that precedes you into every room and follows you out.

**Reputation is not what you say about yourself. It is what other people know about you from experience.** It is built slowly, in drops — every time you show up when you said you would, every time you finish what you committed to, every time you deliver work that meets the standard. And it is lost quickly, in gallons — one betrayal of trust, one dropped commitment, one string of unreliable behavior can undo months of careful building.

This packet teaches you how reputation works, why being known as reliable is one of the most valuable assets you will ever have, what it means to produce work that others depend on, and what it takes to repair trust when you damage it.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Understanding reputation** — how it is built by patterns over time, not by single acts; how it precedes you and follows you
2. **Being known as reliable** — the specific value of being the person others know will finish, show up, and deliver
3. **Producing work others depend on** — the shift from solo work to work that sits inside a larger system
4. **Understanding the cost of unreliability** — what happens to others when you do not deliver, and what happens to your reputation
5. **Repairing trust after mistakes** — what it actually takes to rebuild after you have let someone down
6. **Working on projects with real stakes** — contributing to efforts where your failure has consequences beyond yourself
content.core_content:
### Part 1: What Reputation Actually Is

Reputation is not your Instagram profile. It is not what you say about yourself in an interview. It is not the story you tell. **Reputation is the pattern other people have observed in your behavior over time.**

When someone says "she always comes through," that is reputation. When someone says "I wouldn't count on him for anything important," that is also reputation. Neither of those statements came from a single event. They came from a pattern — dozens of interactions, commitments kept or broken, deadlines met or missed, work delivered at standard or below it.

**Key principles of reputation:**

**Reputation is built by patterns, not by single acts.** One great performance does not make you reliable. One dropped ball does not make you unreliable. What makes you reliable is showing up consistently — twenty times, fifty times, a hundred times — until the people around you stop wondering whether you will deliver and start assuming it. That assumption is reputation.

**Reputation precedes you.** Before you walk into a room, your reputation is already there. When someone recommends you — "ask her, she does great work" — your reputation arrived before you did. When someone warns against you — "I wouldn't trust him with that" — your reputation arrived as a barrier. You do not get to introduce yourself fresh every time. Your track record introduces you.

**Reputation follows you.** When you leave a project, a team, a community, your reputation stays in the conversation. "Remember when he was on the team? He was the one who always..." What fills that blank is your legacy in that context — the residue of your behavior after you are gone.

**Reputation is asymmetric.** This is the most important thing to understand: reputation is built in drops and lost in gallons. It takes months of consistent reliability to build the reputation of being dependable. It takes one significant failure — especially one that looks like carelessness or dishonesty — to damage it severely. And rebuilding after damage takes far longer than the original building did. This asymmetry is not fair. It is real.

### Part 2: Being Known as Reliable

Reliability is the most underrated quality a person can have. It is not flashy. Nobody makes movies about the person who showed up every day and did exactly what they said they would do. But reliability is the quality that makes everything else possible — for you and for everyone who works with you.

**What reliability looks like in practice:**

- **You do what you said you would do.** If you committed to finishing a section of the project by Thursday, it is finished by Thursday. Not "mostly done." Not "I ran out of time." Finished. At standard.
- **You show up when you said you would.** If the meeting is at 3:00, you are there at 3:00. Not 3:10. Not "on my way." There. Lateness is a reliability failure — it tells everyone present that your time matters more than theirs.
- **You deliver at the standard you agreed to.** If the standard is "proofread and clean," that is what you deliver. Not a rough draft you hope no one will notice. The standard was stated. You met it.
- **You communicate when things change.** If something is going to be late, you say so — early, honestly, and with a plan for what you are going to do about it. Silence when a commitment is at risk is worse than the delay itself, because silence prevents others from adjusting.

**Why reliability matters more than talent:**

Here is a truth that most people learn too late: given the choice between a brilliant person who is unreliable and a competent person who is dependable, almost everyone — every employer, every team leader, every partner — will choose the dependable person. Every time.

Talent without reliability is a liability. You can do amazing work — sometimes. When you feel like it. When conditions are right. But the people around you cannot build on that, because they never know when "sometimes" will show up. They cannot depend on you for the critical task, because the risk of you not delivering is too high.

Reliability without exceptional talent still produces results. The person who shows up every day, does solid work, meets their commitments, and never makes others wonder whether the job will get done — that person advances, earns trust, and receives more responsibility. Not because they are the most talented person in the room, but because they are the one everyone knows they can count on.

**The compound effect of reliability:** Each time you deliver on a commitment, you make a small deposit in your reputation. Over time, these deposits compound. People start giving you more responsibility — not as a burden, but as a vote of confidence. You are trusted with larger tasks, more important projects, more autonomy. This is how reliability converts to opportunity. The reliable person gets the interesting work, because the interesting work requires someone who can be counted on.

### Part 3: Work Others Depend On

Until now, most of your work has been solo. You did a project, turned it in, and the main person affected by its quality was you. That changes now.

**When your work sits inside a larger system, everything changes:**

- If you are writing one section of a group report, the quality of the whole report depends partly on the quality of your section. If your section is weak, the whole product is weakened — and everyone's name is on it.
- If you are responsible for one part of a family project — a garden bed, a section of fence, a meal preparation task — the whole project depends on your part being done, on time, at standard.
- If you committed to being somewhere to help someone, their plan depends on your presence. Your absence does not just affect you — it affects them.

**This is the shift from craftsmanship as personal pride to craftsmanship as social responsibility.** You are no longer just producing work you can be proud of. You are producing work that other people are building on. The quality of your output is now a factor in someone else's success or failure.

**What this demands:**

- **Know what others need from you.** Not just what your task is, but how it connects to everyone else's work. What happens downstream if your piece is late? What happens if your piece is substandard? Understanding the system you are part of changes how you approach your own contribution.
- **Deliver to the interface standard.** When your work connects to someone else's, the connection point matters as much as the interior. A beautifully built wall section that does not align with the adjoining section is not good work — it is a problem. Your work must not only be good on its own terms; it must work within the system.
- **Communicate proactively.** If something about your contribution is going to affect the whole, say so early. "I'm running behind on my section" is useful information on Monday. It is a crisis on Friday when the project is due.

### Part 4: The Cost of Unreliability

Unreliability is expensive. Not in money (though sometimes in money too) — in trust, in opportunity, and in the burden it places on others.

**What happens when you are unreliable:**

- **Others have to compensate.** When you do not deliver, someone else has to do your work — or the work does not get done, and everyone suffers the consequence. Every time someone has to cover for you, they are doing their own work plus yours. That breeds resentment, and it should.
- **People stop counting on you.** This happens quietly. You are not fired from the team — you are just no longer given the important tasks. You are not excluded from the project — but your section is the one someone else double-checks. The loss is invisible and devastating: you become the person who is managed around rather than relied upon.
- **Your word loses value.** When you say "I'll have it done by Tuesday," people who have been burned before do not believe you. They do not say this out loud — they just plan as if you will not deliver, because experience has taught them not to trust your commitments. Your words no longer function as commitments. They are just noise.
- **Opportunities stop coming.** The interesting project, the important task, the increased responsibility — these go to the people who have demonstrated they can handle them. Unreliability is a self-imposed ceiling. It does not matter how talented you are if no one trusts you to follow through.

**The hardest part:** Unreliable people often do not know they are unreliable. They have good intentions. They meant to finish. They planned to show up. They thought they would get to it. But intentions are not results. The road between "I planned to" and "I did" is where reliability lives — and most people are not as far along that road as they believe.

### Part 5: Repairing Trust After Mistakes

You will fail. You will drop a commitment. You will deliver late, deliver below standard, or not deliver at all. This is not a hypothetical — it will happen. The question is not whether you will damage trust. The question is what you do afterward.

**What does NOT repair trust:**

- **Excuses.** "I was really busy." "Something came up." "It was not my fault." Excuses explain why you failed. They do not repair the damage. The person who was counting on you does not need to understand your schedule — they needed your deliverable, and they did not get it.
- **Promises without change.** "It won't happen again" is meaningless if the same pattern continues. The second time you say it, people stop hearing it. Trust is rebuilt by behavior, not by assurances.
- **Minimizing.** "It was not that big a deal." To you, maybe. To the person who had to cover your work, rearrange their plan, or explain your absence — it was a deal. Minimizing the impact tells them you do not understand what your failure cost, which makes it likely to happen again.

**What DOES repair trust:**

- **Acknowledge fully.** "I said I would have it done by Thursday. I did not. That put you in a difficult position, and I am sorry." No excuses. No qualifications. Full ownership of the failure and its impact.
- **Make it right.** If the work can still be done, do it — immediately, at a higher standard than originally required, to demonstrate that this matters to you. If it cannot be done, ask: "What can I do now to help?" Not as a token gesture, but as a genuine effort to reduce the damage you caused.
- **Change the behavior.** This is the only thing that actually rebuilds trust over time. Not the apology — the changed pattern. The next commitment is met. The one after that is met. And the one after that. Over time — not quickly, not easily — the new pattern begins to overwrite the old one. But it takes far longer to rebuild than it took to damage.

**The timeline of repair:** If it took you three months of reliability to build trust, and one significant failure to damage it, it will take six months of reliability to rebuild it. Maybe more. That asymmetry — build in drops, lose in gallons — applies to repair as well. This is not unfair. This is the real cost of unreliability, and understanding it before you fail is part of what IC2.5-B teaches.

### Part 6: Projects With Real Stakes

Everything in Parts 1 through 5 becomes real when you are working on something that matters — where the consequences of your performance extend beyond you.

**What "real stakes" means:**

- Someone else is depending on your work to complete theirs
- A deadline is not arbitrary — something happens (or fails to happen) if it is missed
- The quality of the output will be seen, used, or evaluated by people whose opinion matters
- Failure has a consequence beyond "you get a lower grade" — someone is inconvenienced, a project is incomplete, a system does not work

**At this tier, real-stakes projects should be part of your regular experience:**

- Family projects where your assigned section is genuinely necessary — not make-work, but a real contribution that the project cannot succeed without
- Group work with peers where the deliverable has a real audience or real use
- Commitments to people outside your family — volunteer work, community projects, obligations to mentors or instructors
- Responsibilities that escalate as you demonstrate reliability — more significant tasks, more autonomy, more trust

**Why real stakes matter for formation:** Practice without consequences teaches technique but not character. You can practice reliability in low-stakes settings forever and still not know whether it will hold when the pressure is real. IC2.5-B requires that you experience what it feels like to have someone counting on you — and to discover whether your habits of care, your commitment to finishing, and your standard of quality survive that pressure.
content.formative_practices:
### Practice 1: The Commitment Log [Daily]
In your project journal, track every commitment you make — to yourself, to family members, to team members, to anyone. Write down what you committed to, when it is due, and the standard it needs to meet. When the deadline arrives, record the result: delivered on time and at standard? Delivered late? Delivered below standard? Not delivered? This log is the raw data of your reliability. Review it weekly. The numbers do not lie.

### Practice 2: The Weekly Reliability Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
Once a week, review your commitment log with your mentor. Calculate your delivery rate — what percentage of your commitments were met, on time, at standard? Discuss: Where did you fall short? What was the cause — poor planning, poor execution, overcommitment, or something else? What is your plan for next week? This review is not punishment. It is calibration — the same principle as the time-estimation exercise in IC1.1-B1, applied to reliability.

### Practice 3: The Real-Stakes Project [Ongoing]
You should always have at least one project where someone else depends on your contribution. This could be:
- A family project where your section is genuinely needed
- A group project with peers where you own a deliverable
- A recurring responsibility (caring for animals, maintaining equipment, managing a household system) that others rely on
The project must have real consequences for failure — not punitive consequences, but natural ones: the project is incomplete, the system does not work, someone else has to cover.

### Practice 4: Reputation Observation [Monthly]
Once a month, have a conversation with your mentor about reputation — not yours specifically, but the concept. Discuss: Who do you know (in real life or from reading) who has a strong reputation for reliability? What built it? Who has a weak reputation? What caused it? What would it take to change? These conversations build the framework for understanding reputation as a real phenomenon, not an abstract concept.

### Practice 5: The Repair Practice [As Needed]
When you fail to meet a commitment — and you will — practice the full repair cycle: acknowledge the failure fully (to the person affected, not just to yourself), make it right if possible, and identify the behavior change needed to prevent recurrence. Document this in your commitment log. The repair is as much a part of the practice as the commitment itself.

### Practice 6: Responsibility Escalation [Quarterly]
Every quarter, review with your mentor whether your demonstrated reliability warrants increased responsibility. If your commitment log shows consistent delivery, you should be given more significant tasks — with more autonomy and higher stakes. If your log shows inconsistency, the responsibility level holds where it is until the pattern improves. This is not a reward/punishment system — it is the natural consequence of demonstrated capacity.

### Practice 7: Craft Showcase [Twice Yearly]
Twice a year, gather your best work from the preceding period — projects, writings, built objects, anything you produced — and present it. Not just display it, but explain it: what you made, what standard you held, what was difficult, and what you would do differently. This is practice for Standing Ground's portfolio and signature project concepts. It builds the habit of standing behind your work publicly.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Creating Real Stakes

IC2.5-B cannot be taught through worksheets or hypothetical scenarios. The student must experience what it feels like to have someone genuinely depending on their work — and to discover what happens when they deliver and what happens when they do not.

**How to create real-stakes contexts:**

- **Family projects with genuine interdependence.** Not make-work assigned to the child so they feel included — actual projects where the child's section is necessary for the whole to succeed. Building a fence where the child is responsible for one section. Preparing a meal where the child owns one course. Planning a trip where the child is responsible for researching and preparing one component. The key criterion: if the child does not deliver, the project is genuinely incomplete or degraded.

- **Group work with peers.** If the student is part of a co-op, a club, a sports team, a community group, or any context where they work alongside others, ensure they have specific deliverables that the group depends on. Not "helping out" — owning a piece. If no formal group exists, create one: a project with siblings, a joint effort with a friend's family, a community service commitment with defined individual responsibilities.

- **Commitments to people outside the family.** A mentor, an instructor, a neighbor, a community member — someone the child has made a promise to and who will notice if it is not kept. Family members often compensate silently when a child fails to deliver. External commitments are less forgiving, and that lack of forgiveness is formative.

- **Responsibility that escalates.** As the student demonstrates reliability, increase what they are trusted with. This is not a reward system — it is a natural consequence of demonstrated capacity. The student who reliably maintains one garden bed is given a second. The student who consistently meets their section deadline on a group project is given a more critical section next time. The escalation itself communicates: "Your reliability has been observed, and it has earned you this."

### The Commitment Log as Data Source

The commitment log described in the student packet is the single most important tool in IC2.5-B. Without it, all conversations about reliability are subjective — "I think I'm pretty reliable" versus "your log shows you met 60% of your commitments last month."

**Your role with the commitment log:**

- **Ensure it exists and is maintained daily.** The log is non-negotiable. Every commitment the student makes — to you, to family, to peers, to themselves — is recorded. The deadline and standard are recorded. The result is recorded.
- **Do not maintain it for them.** The log is the student's responsibility. If they fail to maintain it, that failure is itself a reliability data point worth discussing.
- **Use it as the basis for the weekly review.** The numbers in the log are the conversation. Not your subjective impression, not the student's self-assessment — the data. "You made fourteen commitments this week. You met ten. Four were missed. Let's look at why."
- **Track trends, not individual data points.** A single missed commitment is not a crisis. A pattern of missed commitments in a specific category (e.g., always late on writing deadlines, always reliable on physical tasks) reveals something useful about where the student's reliability breaks down.

### Modeling Reliability

As with every IC packet, the mentor's behavior is the curriculum. If you make commitments to the student and break them — "we'll go to the park Saturday" that becomes "something came up" — you are teaching that commitments are provisional. If you are late to things you scheduled with the student, you are teaching that other people's time does not matter.

This does not mean performing perfection. It means that when you fail — and you will — you model the repair cycle explicitly: "I told you I would have that ready by today and I do not. That is my failure, not yours. I am going to have it done by tomorrow. I am sorry for the delay." The student needs to see that adults take their own reliability seriously, and that the repair process applies to everyone, not just to children.
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Understanding of Reputation

**Developing well:** The student can explain how reputation is formed — through patterns of behavior over time, not single acts. They can give concrete examples from their own life or from reading. They understand the asymmetry: built in drops, lost in gallons. They apply the concept to real situations, not just recite it.

**Developing slowly:** The student defines reputation as "what people think of you" without understanding the mechanism. They cannot connect their own daily behavior to the reputation they are building. The concept remains abstract.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough real-stakes experience for reputation to be tangible? (A student working entirely solo on low-stakes tasks has no context for reputation.) Have you discussed specific examples — real people the student knows, characters from reading — where reputation was built or damaged? (The concept needs narrative anchors.)

### 2. Reliability Rate

**Developing well:** The commitment log shows an improving trend. The student meets a high percentage of commitments (70%+ and rising) on time and at standard. When they miss, the misses are acknowledged and the causes are identifiable and being addressed.

**Developing slowly:** The commitment log shows persistent low delivery rates without improvement. The same failure patterns repeat without change. Alternatively, the student is not maintaining the log at all — which is itself a reliability failure.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the commitments realistic? (A student who overcommits and then fails 50% of the time may have a planning problem, not a reliability problem. Address the overcommitment first — IC1.1-B1 territory.) Is the student receiving natural consequences for failure? (If missed commitments produce no consequence — the parent compensates, the deadline is extended, the standard is lowered — there is no feedback signal to drive improvement.) Is the student getting enough sleep, managing their time adequately? (Chronic fatigue and poor time management are the most common structural causes of unreliability at this age.)

### 3. Proactive Communication

**Developing well:** When a commitment is at risk, the student communicates early — before the deadline, not after. They identify the problem, state the impact, and propose a solution or revised timeline. This is one of the strongest indicators of reputation awareness, because it shows the student understands that others are planning around their commitment.

**Developing slowly:** The student goes silent when a deadline is at risk. The failure is discovered only when the deadline passes. Alternatively, the student communicates but only to make excuses rather than to solve the problem.

**How to build this:** Make proactive communication the explicit expectation. "If something is going to be late, I need to know before it is late, not after. Tell me as soon as you know there is a risk." Then respond well when they do communicate early — not with punishment for the potential miss, but with problem-solving. If early communication is punished ("you should have planned better"), the student learns to stay silent. If it is met with collaboration, the student learns that communication is a tool for managing reliability, not an admission of failure.

### 4. Work That Serves Others

**Developing well:** The student has experience producing work that others depend on, and has delivered. They can describe what it felt like to know someone was counting on them, and how it changed their approach to the work. They understand the connection between their output and the system it sits within.

**Developing slowly:** The student's work experience is entirely solo. They have not yet felt the weight of someone else's expectation. Or they have had the experience and failed to deliver, without recognizing the impact on others.

**How to build this:** Engineer interdependent projects. The student's contribution must be genuinely necessary — not a token addition that the group could survive without. The consequences of failure must be real and observable. And the student must receive feedback from the people who depended on them, not just from the mentor.

### 5. Trust Repair

**Developing well:** When the student fails to deliver, they initiate the repair process: full acknowledgment, no excuses, an offer to make it right, and a visible change in behavior going forward. They understand that repair takes time and continued performance, not just an apology.

**Developing slowly:** Failures are met with excuses, minimization, or silence. The student expects a verbal apology to resolve the situation and is surprised when trust is not immediately restored. The same failure pattern repeats without behavior change.

**How to build this:** The first time the student fails significantly, walk through the repair process explicitly. Not as a punishment script, but as a genuine skill: "Here is what happened. Here is what it cost the other person. Here is what acknowledgment looks like. Here is what making it right looks like. Here is what changing the behavior looks like." Then give the student space to do it. The repair must be theirs — you cannot do it for them.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Protecting the Student From Consequences
The most damaging thing a mentor can do in IC2.5-B is shield the student from the natural consequences of unreliability. When the student fails to deliver their section of a group project, and the mentor stays up late completing it for them, the student learns that failure has no cost. When the student misses a commitment to a neighbor, and the mentor apologizes on their behalf without requiring the student to face the person, the student learns that other people will handle the fallout of their unreliability.

Let the consequences land. Not punitively — you do not need to add consequences. The natural ones are sufficient: the project is incomplete, the person is disappointed, the responsibility is not given again. These are the signals that drive formation.

### Lecturing About Reputation Instead of Engineering Experience
Reputation cannot be taught through conversation alone. A fourteen-year-old who has been told that "reputation matters" but has never experienced the consequences of their own reliability or unreliability in a real context has learned nothing useful. The conversations matter — but only when they are grounded in specific, recent, personal experience. "Your commitment log shows you met your deadline three weeks in a row and Mrs. Johnson asked if you could take on a larger section of the garden project. That is your reputation producing opportunity." This is a hundred times more formative than a lecture about reputation in the abstract.

### Treating the Commitment Log as Punishment
The log must be framed as a tool, not as a surveillance mechanism. "We are tracking this so you can see your own patterns and improve" — not "we are tracking this so I can prove you are unreliable." If the student experiences the log as evidence being gathered against them, they will resist it, undercount their commitments, or stop maintaining it. The log is for the student's self-awareness first, and for the mentor-student conversation second.

### Overcommitment Without Correction
Some students — especially eager, people-pleasing ones — will commit to more than they can deliver. Their commitment log will show many commitments and many failures, not because they are careless but because they cannot say no. This is a planning problem (IC1.1-B1) more than a reliability problem (IC2.5-B). Address the root cause: help the student learn to assess their capacity honestly before committing, and to say "I cannot take that on right now" when they are at capacity. An honest "no" is more reliable than a dishonest "yes."

### Confusing Compliance With Reliability
A student who does what they are told, when they are told, is compliant. A student who identifies what needs to be done, commits to it, and delivers without being managed is reliable. Compliance is externally driven; reliability is internally driven. IC2.5-B aims for the latter. If the student only delivers when you are actively monitoring, the reliability is not yet internalized — it is managed. The test: what happens when you are not watching?
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What Is Reputation?
**Strong:** Defines reputation as a pattern observed by others over time. Gives a specific example — a real person or a character from reading — and can describe how their reputation was built through consistent behavior. Understands the asymmetry (built slowly, damaged quickly).
**Weak:** "What people think of me." No mechanism, no examples, no understanding of how it forms.

### Prompt 2: Are You Reliable?
**Strong:** Answers with reference to their commitment log data. Identifies specific areas of strength and weakness. Acknowledges the gap between self-perception and actual performance if one exists. This is the most revealing prompt because it tests honest self-assessment.
**Weak:** "Yes" without evidence, or "I try" without specifics. No reference to actual data.

### Prompt 3: Work as Part of Something Larger
**Strong:** Describes a specific project where their contribution was genuinely needed. Can explain how their piece connected to the whole, and what would have happened if they had not delivered. Shows awareness of interdependence.
**Weak:** Cannot recall such an experience, or describes one where their contribution was not genuinely necessary ("I helped").

### Prompt 4: Letting Someone Down
**Strong:** Describes a specific instance honestly. Names the cost to the other person, not just to themselves. Describes what they did (or did not do) to repair. Shows genuine understanding of the impact. This is the hardest prompt and the most formative — it requires vulnerability and honesty.
**Weak:** "I don't think I have." (Either dishonest or lacking the self-awareness to recognize their failures.) Or describes the event but focuses on their own feelings rather than the impact on the other person.

### Prompt 5: The Most Reliable Person You Know
**Strong:** Names a specific person and provides specific behavioral evidence. "My grandfather — he said he would help build the shed, and he was there every Saturday at 8 AM for six weeks. He never missed once." The specificity reveals whether the student understands what reliability actually looks like in practice.
**Weak:** "My mom" without explanation. No behavioral evidence. No connection to the broader concept.

### Prompt 6: Excuse vs. Explanation
**Strong:** Can articulate the difference: an excuse deflects responsibility; an acknowledgment accepts it. An excuse focuses on why the failure was not really the person's fault; an acknowledgment focuses on the impact and the path forward. The student can identify when they have offered an excuse versus a genuine acknowledgment.
**Weak:** Cannot distinguish, or defines both as "saying sorry."
